ch01.definition: The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom.
The total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus of an atom.
Atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
The weighted mean mass of an atom of an element relative to 1/12 the mass of a carbon-12 atom.
The mass of an isotope relative to 1/12 the mass of a carbon-12 atom.
The energy required to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms to form one mole of gaseous 1+ ions: X(g) → X+(g) + e−
The energies required to remove electrons one by one from a gaseous ion, each successive electron being removed from an increasingly positive ion.
A region of space that can hold up to two electrons with opposite spins.
The arrangement of electrons in an atom, showing the number of electrons in each sub-shell (e.g. 1s² 2s² 2p⁶).
The spectrum produced when excited electrons fall to lower energy levels, emitting photons of specific frequencies.
The lowest energy electron configuration of an atom.
The repulsion between two electrons occupying the same orbital, causing a slight increase in energy.

ch03.definition: The sum of the relative atomic masses of all atoms in a molecule.
The sum of the relative atomic masses of all atoms in the empirical formula of a compound (used for ionic compounds).
The amount of substance containing as many particles as there are atoms in exactly 12 g of carbon-12 (6.02 × 10²³ particles).
The number of particles in one mole of substance: 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹.
The mass per mole of a substance, numerically equal to its relative molecular mass, in g mol⁻¹.
The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound.
The actual number of atoms of each element in one molecule of a compound.
A formula showing the arrangement of atoms and bonds in a molecule.
The volume occupied by one mole of a gas at a given temperature and pressure (24.0 dm³ at RTP).
The quantitative relationship between reactants and products in a chemical reaction, as given by the balanced equation.
The reactant that is completely consumed in a reaction, limiting the amount of product formed.
The ratio of actual yield to theoretical yield, expressed as a percentage.
A measure of the proportion of starting materials that end up as useful products: (Mr of desired product / Mr of all products) × 100%.

ch04.definition: The electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by the transfer of electrons.
A bond formed by the sharing of a pair of electrons between two atoms.
A covalent bond in which both electrons are donated by the same atom.
The electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a sea of delocalised electrons.
The average distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms.
The energy required to break one mole of a particular bond in gaseous molecules.
A measure of the tendency of an atom to attract a bonding pair of electrons towards itself.
A covalent bond in which there is an unequal distribution of electron density due to a difference in electronegativity between the two atoms.
A measure of the polarity of a bond or molecule, equal to the product of the charge and the distance between the charges.
Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion theory: electron pairs around a central atom arrange themselves to minimise repulsion.
A bond formed by end-on overlap of atomic orbitals, with electron density concentrated along the internuclear axis.
A bond formed by sideways overlap of p orbitals, with electron density above and below the internuclear axis.
The mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals of equal energy and shape.
Weak intermolecular forces caused by temporary induced dipoles (also called London dispersion forces).
A strong dipole-dipole attraction between a hydrogen atom bonded to a highly electronegative atom (N, O, or F) and a lone pair on another electronegative atom.
Intermolecular forces between molecules with permanent dipoles, where the positive end of one molecule attracts the negative end of another.

ch05.definition: A gas that obeys the ideal gas equation (pV = nRT) under all conditions; its molecules have no volume and no intermolecular forces.
pV = nRT, where p = pressure (Pa), V = volume (m³), n = moles, R = gas constant (8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹), T = temperature (K).
A crystalline structure in which molecules are held together by weak van der Waals forces in a regular arrangement.
A structure in which a large number of atoms are covalently bonded in a continuous three-dimensional network.
A crystalline structure consisting of a regular arrangement of oppositely charged ions held together by electrostatic forces.
A structure consisting of a regular arrangement of positive metal ions surrounded by delocalised electrons.
The existence of two or more different physical forms (allotropes) of the same element.

ch06.definition: The heat energy change at constant pressure during a chemical reaction.
The enthalpy change measured under standard conditions (298 K, 100 kPa) with all substances in their standard states.
The enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its elements in their standard states under standard conditions.
The enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is completely burned in excess oxygen under standard conditions.
The enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous atoms is formed from the element in its standard state.
The total enthalpy change of a reaction is independent of the route taken, provided the initial and final conditions are the same.
The energy required to break one mole of a covalent bond in gaseous molecules, averaged over a range of compounds.
An experimental technique used to measure enthalpy changes by measuring temperature changes in a known mass of water.
A reaction that releases energy to the surroundings, resulting in a negative ΔH value.
A reaction that absorbs energy from the surroundings, resulting in a positive ΔH value.

ch07.definition: The loss of electrons, or an increase in oxidation number.
The gain of electrons, or a decrease in oxidation number.
A substance that oxidises another substance and is itself reduced (gains electrons).
A substance that reduces another substance and is itself oxidised (loses electrons).
A number assigned to an atom to indicate the degree of oxidation; the charge the atom would have if the compound were fully ionic.
A reaction in which both oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously.
A redox reaction in which the same species is simultaneously oxidised and reduced.
An equation showing either the oxidation or the reduction process in a redox reaction, including electrons.

ch08.definition: A state in which the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction, and the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant.
For a reaction aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD, Kc = [C]^c[D]^d / ([A]^a[B]^b), where [ ] denotes equilibrium concentration in mol dm⁻³.
The equilibrium constant expressed in terms of partial pressures of gases.
When a system in dynamic equilibrium is subjected to a change, the equilibrium shifts to minimise the effect of that change.
The pressure that a gas in a mixture would exert if it alone occupied the same volume at the same temperature.
The ratio of the number of moles of one component to the total number of moles in a mixture.

ch09.definition: The change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time.
An experimentally determined equation showing how the rate of a reaction depends on the concentrations of reactants: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n.
The proportionality constant in the rate equation; its value depends on temperature.
The power to which the concentration of a reactant is raised in the rate equation.
The time taken for the concentration of a reactant to fall to half its initial value.
The minimum energy required by colliding particles to react.
A substance that increases the rate of a reaction by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy, and is not consumed in the reaction.
A distribution showing the range of energies of particles in a sample at a given temperature.
Catalysis in which the catalyst and reactants are in the same phase.
Catalysis in which the catalyst and reactants are in different phases.

ch10.definition: The regular variation in properties of elements across a period and down a group of the Periodic Table.
The energy required to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms or ions.
Half the distance between the nuclei of two adjacent atoms of the same element.
A measure of the tendency of an atom to attract a bonding electron pair; increases across a period and decreases down a group.
Across Period 3: increases from Na to Al (metallic), peaks at Si (giant covalent), then drops sharply for P, S, Cl (simple molecular).

ch11.definition: The alkaline earth metals (Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba) in Group 2 of the Periodic Table; they form 2+ ions.
Group 2 carbonates decompose on heating: MCO₃ → MO + CO₂. Stability increases down the group.
Group 2 metals react with water to form hydroxides and hydrogen: M + 2H₂O → M(OH)₂ + H₂. Reactivity increases down the group.
Solubility increases down Group 2: Mg(OH)₂ is sparingly soluble; Ba(OH)₂ is soluble.
Solubility decreases down Group 2: MgSO₄ is soluble; BaSO₄ is insoluble.

ch12.definition: The elements F, Cl, Br, I in Group 17; they exist as diatomic molecules and form 1− ions.
Reactivity decreases down Group 17 as atomic radius increases and electron affinity decreases.
Cl₂ + H₂O → HCl + HClO (in cold water); Cl₂ + 2NaOH → NaCl + NaClO + H₂O.
Reducing power increases down Group 17: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻; F⁻ cannot act as a reducing agent.
Add dilute HNO₃ then AgNO₃ solution: AgCl (white), AgBr (cream), AgI (yellow) precipitates form.

ch13.definition: The continuous recycling of nitrogen through the atmosphere, soil, and living organisms via processes such as fixation, nitrification, and denitrification.
Industrial synthesis of ammonia: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃; conditions: 450°C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst.
Industrial synthesis of sulfuric acid via SO₂ → SO₃ (V₂O₅ catalyst, 450°C), then SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇, then H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → H₂SO₄.
Rain with pH below 5.6 caused by dissolution of SO₂ and NOₓ gases from burning fossil fuels.
A device in car exhausts that converts CO, NOₓ, and unburnt hydrocarbons into CO₂, N₂, and H₂O using Pt/Rh/Pd catalysts.

ch14.definition: A series of organic compounds with the same functional group and general formula, differing by a CH₂ unit.
An atom or group of atoms responsible for the characteristic chemical reactions of a compound.
Compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements of atoms.
Compounds with the same molecular formula and structural formula but different spatial arrangements of atoms.
A type of stereoisomerism arising from restricted rotation about a C=C double bond, requiring two different groups on each carbon.
A type of stereoisomerism arising from a chiral centre (an asymmetric carbon with four different groups), producing non-superimposable mirror images (enantiomers).
A carbon atom bonded to four different groups, giving rise to optical isomers.
Bond breaking in which each atom receives one electron from the shared pair, producing radicals.
Bond breaking in which both electrons go to one atom, producing ions (carbocations or carbanions).
An electron-rich species that donates a pair of electrons to form a new bond; attacks electron-deficient centres.
An electron-deficient species that accepts a pair of electrons to form a new bond; attacks electron-rich centres.
A species with one or more unpaired electrons, formed by homolytic fission.

ch15.definition: A saturated hydrocarbon with the general formula CnH₂n+₂; contains only C-C and C-H single bonds.
An unsaturated hydrocarbon with the general formula CnH₂n; contains at least one C=C double bond.
A reaction in which an electrophile adds across a double bond, proceeding through a carbocation intermediate.
In addition to an unsymmetrical alkene, the hydrogen adds to the carbon with more hydrogen atoms (the major product arises from the more stable carbocation).
A chain reaction in alkanes with halogens in UV light, proceeding through initiation, propagation, and termination steps.
Complete combustion: CₓHᵧ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O. Incomplete: produces CO and/or C (soot).
The breaking down of large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller, more useful ones by heat (thermal cracking) or catalysts (catalytic cracking).

ch16.definition: An organic compound containing one or more halogen atoms bonded to a carbon chain.
A substitution reaction proceeding via a carbocation intermediate; rate depends only on the concentration of the halogenoalkane.
A substitution reaction proceeding via a concerted mechanism (backside attack); rate depends on concentrations of both the halogenoalkane and the nucleophile.
A reaction in which a small molecule (e.g. HBr) is removed from a halogenoalkane to form an alkene, using hot ethanolic KOH.
Rate increases: C–F < C–Cl < C–Br < C–I, because bond enthalpy decreases down the group.
Chlorofluorocarbons; stable compounds used as refrigerants that decompose in the stratosphere by UV light to produce Cl radicals, which catalytically destroy ozone.

ch17.definition: An organic compound containing the –OH (hydroxyl) functional group.
An alcohol in which the carbon bearing the –OH group is bonded to one other carbon (–CH₂OH).
An alcohol in which the carbon bearing the –OH group is bonded to two other carbons.
An alcohol in which the carbon bearing the –OH group is bonded to three other carbons.
Primary alcohols can be oxidised to aldehydes (then carboxylic acids); secondary to ketones; tertiary alcohols resist oxidation. Reagent: K₂Cr₂O₇ / H₂SO₄.
A reversible reaction between a carboxylic acid and an alcohol (with acid catalyst) to form an ester and water.
A compound formed by condensation of a carboxylic acid and an alcohol, with the functional group –COO–.
The alkaline hydrolysis of an ester to produce a carboxylate salt and an alcohol.
An organic compound containing the –COOH (carboxyl) functional group.

ch18.definition: A carbonyl compound (–CHO) in which the carbonyl group is at the end of a carbon chain.
A carbonyl compound (–CO–) in which the carbonyl group is between two carbon atoms.
A reaction in which a nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon of a carbonyl group, adding across the C=O bond.
A test to distinguish aldehydes from ketones: aldehydes reduce Cu²⁺ (blue) to Cu₂O (brick-red precipitate); ketones do not react.
Ammoniacal silver nitrate solution; aldehydes produce a silver mirror; ketones do not react.
Aldehydes are reduced to primary alcohols; ketones to secondary alcohols. Reagent: NaBH₄ or LiAlH₄.
Nucleophilic addition of HCN to an aldehyde or ketone to form a hydroxynitrile (cyanohydrin).

ch19.definition: The enthalpy change when one mole of an ionic compound is formed from its constituent gaseous ions (always exothermic, negative value). Also defined as the reverse process (always positive).
An energy cycle used to calculate lattice energy, using Hess's Law and enthalpy changes including atomisation, ionisation, electron affinity, and formation.
The enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous atoms gains one mole of electrons to form one mole of gaseous 1− ions.
The enthalpy change when one mole of gaseous ions is completely surrounded by water molecules.
The enthalpy change when one mole of an ionic compound dissolves in a large excess of water: ΔHsol = ΔHlat + ΔHhyd (ions).
Distortion of the electron cloud of an anion by a small, highly charged cation; increases covalent character of the bond.

ch20.definition: The potential difference between an electrode and a standard hydrogen electrode under standard conditions.
The potential of an electrode measured relative to the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) under standard conditions (298 K, 1 mol dm⁻³, 100 kPa).
The reference electrode (E° = 0.00 V): Pt | H₂(g, 1 atm) | H⁺(aq, 1 mol dm⁻³).
E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode = E°(more positive) − E°(less positive).
The decomposition of a substance by passing an electric current through its molten or aqueous state.
The amount of substance discharged at an electrode is proportional to the quantity of charge passed: Q = It = nzF.
A redox reaction is spontaneous if E°cell > 0 (the species with the more positive E° is reduced).

ch21.definition: Two species that differ by a single proton (H⁺).
pH = −log₁₀[H⁺]; a measure of the acidity of a solution.
Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA]; a measure of the strength of a weak acid.
pKa = −log₁₀(Ka); the negative logarithm of Ka.
A solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added, typically consisting of a weak acid and its conjugate base.
The reaction of an ion with water; a salt of a weak acid and strong base gives an alkaline solution; salt of strong acid and weak base gives an acidic solution.
Weak acids (HIn) where the undissociated form (HIn) and the dissociate form (In⁻) have different colours; colour change occurs near pKa.

ch22.definition: The slowest step in a reaction mechanism, which determines the overall rate of the reaction.
The number of species involved in the rate-determining step of a reaction.
k = Ae^(−Ea/RT); describes the temperature dependence of the rate constant.
The pre-exponential factor in the Arrhenius equation; related to the frequency of collisions with correct orientation.
For first order: [A] = [A]₀e^(−kt), t½ = ln2/k. For second order: 1/[A] = 1/[A]₀ + kt.
A method to determine reaction order by comparing rates at different initial concentrations.

ch23.definition: A measure of the disorder or randomness of a system; increases when disorder increases.
ΔS° = ΣS°(products) − ΣS°(reactants); measured in J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.
ΔG = ΔH − TΔS; a thermodynamic quantity that determines whether a reaction is spontaneous.
A reaction is spontaneous when ΔG < 0 at constant temperature and pressure.
ΔG° = −RT ln K; relates the standard free energy change to the equilibrium constant.
The temperature at which ΔG = 0: T = ΔH/ΔS. Above/below this temperature the reaction becomes spontaneous, depending on the signs of ΔH and ΔS.

ch24.definition: A d-block element that forms at least one stable ion with an incomplete d sub-shell.
Transition elements exhibit multiple oxidation states because the 3d and 4s electrons have similar energies.
A metal ion surrounded by ligands (Lewis bases) that donate lone pairs to the metal.
A molecule or ion that donates a lone pair of electrons to a metal ion to form a coordinate (dative) bond.
The number of coordinate bonds formed between ligands and the central metal ion.
The absorption of visible light causing an electron to move from a lower to a higher d orbital; responsible for the colour of transition metal complexes.
Transition metal ions with unpaired d electrons are paramagnetic (attracted to a magnetic field).
Transition metals and their compounds act as catalysts by providing a surface for reactants and by changing oxidation state.
A reaction in which one or more ligands in a complex ion are replaced by other ligands.
The equilibrium constant for the formation of a complex ion from its constituent metal ion and ligands in aqueous solution.

ch25.definition: A compound containing a benzene ring; benzene (C₆H₆) has a delocalized π electron system.
The six π electrons in benzene are spread equally over all six carbon atoms, giving extra stability (delocalisation energy).
A reaction of benzene in which an electrophile substitutes a hydrogen atom on the ring, preserving the aromatic system.
Electrophilic substitution using an alkyl halide or acyl halide and an AlCl₃ catalyst to introduce alkyl or acyl groups to benzene.
Benzene + HNO₃ (concentrated) + H₂SO₄ (concentrated) at 55°C → nitrobenzene + H₂O. Electrophile: NO₂⁺ (nitronium ion).
Existing substituents on benzene direct incoming electrophiles: –OH, –NH₂ are 2,4-directors (activating); –NO₂, –COOH are 3-directors (deactivating).

ch26.definition: A compound with the functional group –COCl; highly reactive, undergoes nucleophilic addition-elimination.
A compound formed from two carboxylic acid molecules with loss of water (–CO–O–CO–); less reactive than acyl chlorides.
A reaction in which a nucleophile adds to a carbonyl group, followed by elimination of a leaving group, producing an acyl substitution product.
Acyl chloride + alcohol → ester + HCl (rapid, room temperature, no catalyst needed).
Acyl chloride + amine → amide + HCl; acid anhydride + amine → amide + carboxylic acid.

ch27.definition: An organic compound derived from ammonia by replacing one or more hydrogen atoms with alkyl or aryl groups; contains –NH₂, –NHR, or –NR₂.
Amines are bases because the lone pair on nitrogen accepts H⁺. Alkyl amines are stronger bases than ammonia; aryl amines are weaker.
A compound containing the –CONH– group; formed from carboxylic acid derivatives and amines.
A compound with both an amino (–NH₂) and a carboxyl (–COOH) group; they exist as zwitterions at their isoelectric point.
The amide bond (–CO–NH–) formed between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of another, with loss of water.
A compound R–N⁺≡N, formed by diazotisation of a primary aryl amine with NaNO₂/HCl at 0–5°C.
A reaction between a diazonium salt and a phenol or aryl amine to form an azo compound (–N=N– group), used to make dyes.

ch28.definition: A reaction in which many alkene monomers join together to form a long chain polymer, with no other products.
A reaction in which monomers join together with the elimination of a small molecule (usually H₂O or HCl) to form a polymer.
A condensation polymer formed from a diol and a dicarboxylic acid (or diester), linked by ester bonds (–COO–).
A condensation polymer formed from a diamine and a dicarboxylic acid (or diacyl chloride), linked by amide bonds (–CONH–).
The ability of a polymer to be broken down by microorganisms; condensation polymers (e.g. polyesters, polyamides) are generally biodegradable; addition polymers are not.
The smallest repeating structural unit in a polymer chain.

ch29.definition: The construction of complex organic molecules from simpler starting materials through a sequence of chemical reactions.
Reactions used to transform one functional group into another in a synthesis route.
A temporary group added to a functional group to prevent it from reacting during subsequent steps of a synthesis.
Overall yield = product of fractional yields of each step; short routes with high-yielding steps are preferred.
Working backwards from the target molecule to identify suitable starting materials and reagents.

ch30.definition: A technique that determines the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) of ions; used to find relative molecular mass (molecular ion M⁺) and structural information from fragmentation patterns.
The ion formed by removal of one electron from a molecule in a mass spectrometer; its m/z gives the relative molecular mass.
A technique that identifies functional groups based on absorption of IR radiation at characteristic wavenumbers (e.g. O–H: 2500–3300 cm⁻¹; C=O: 1700–1750 cm⁻¹).
Nuclear Magnetic Resonance spectroscopy identifies the environment of ¹H or ¹³C nuclei in a molecule; chemical shift (δ, in ppm) indicates the type of proton/carbon.
The position of an NMR absorption relative to TMS (tetramethylsilane, δ = 0); different proton environments give different δ values.
The splitting of NMR peaks caused by neighbouring non-equivalent protons; (n+1) rule: n adjacent protons cause splitting into n+1 peaks.
A separation technique based on differential partitioning between a mobile phase and a stationary phase.
In TLC/paper chromatography, Rf = distance travelled by spot / distance travelled by solvent front.
Emission spectroscopy used to identify and quantify metal ions (especially Na, K, Li, Ca) based on characteristic flame colours.
Absorbance (A) = εcl, where ε = molar absorptivity, c = concentration, l = path length; used in colorimetry/UV-visible spectroscopy.
